1: A theme: YHWH commands departure toward the promised land. Christ leads his people to their inheritance (Hebrews 3:6; 1 Peter 1:3-5), and his promise in Matthew 28:20 — 'I am with you always' — reverses the withdrawal announced in the following verses.
2: A shadow: an angel (mal'ak) sent before them. The angel of YHWH in Ex 3:2 was identified with YHWH himself; Christ is not a mere envoy but the Presence itself (John 1:14; Colossians 1:15-17). Revelation 22:16 names the risen Christ as the one who sends the messenger.
3: A theme: 'I will not go up in your midst, lest I consume you, for you are a stiff-necked people.' The withdrawal of divine Presence because of sin is the exact problem the incarnation resolves. Christ enters human community not to consume but to save (Matthew 1:21; Luke 9:56). Hebrews 10:19-22 now opens the Most Holy Place for all who come through him.
4: A theme: Israel mourned when they heard this troubling word; no one put on ornaments. The absence of God's Presence is mourning itself. Matthew 9:15: 'Can the wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them?' The presence of Christ reverses the cause of mourning (Revelation 21:4).
5: A revelation of God: 'You are a stiff-necked people; if I go with you for even a moment I would consume you.' The terror of divine holiness that cannot dwell with unrepentant sin. Christ as the Lamb of God absorbs this consuming judgment so that sinners may stand (John 1:29; 2 Corinthians 5:21; Hebrews 9:14).
6: A theme: Israel stripped off their ornaments at Horeb — a gesture of mourning and acknowledged unworthiness. Luke 15:17-24: the returning prodigal relinquishes all claim to status and is clothed by the father's grace. Unworthiness is met not with rejection but with the covering of the mediator.
7: A type: the tent of meeting (ohel mo'ed) placed outside the camp, away from the sinful community. Hebrews 13:12-13: 'Jesus suffered outside the gate... let us go to him outside the camp, bearing the disgrace he bore.' The meeting place of the new covenant is outside the center of human social respectability — Christ crucified outside Jerusalem's walls.
8: A theme: the people rose and watched Moses until he had entered the tent. The community watching the mediator approach God on their behalf. 1 Timothy 2:5: 'there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus.' Hebrews 9:24: Christ 'entered heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf.'
9: A type: the pillar of cloud descended at the tent entrance when Moses entered; YHWH spoke with Moses. The cloud as the Spirit's presence (1 Corinthians 10:1-2). At Jesus's baptism the Spirit descends as a dove and the Father speaks (Matthew 3:16-17), making the same pattern explicit at the new covenant's inauguration.
10: A theme: all the people rose and worshiped at their tent doors when the cloud descended. The visible presence of God with the mediator draws the whole community to worship. Revelation 5:11-14: every creature worshiping the Lamb who has made access to the Presence possible.
11: A type and its limit: 'YHWH spoke to Moses face to face, as a man speaks with his friend.' Moses is the greatest OT type of Christ as mediator, yet John 1:18 marks the type's boundary: 'No one has seen God at any time; the only-begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, has made him known.' Christ does not speak with God as a friend — he speaks from within the divine life as God the Son.
12: A type: Moses intercedes on the basis of divine intimacy — 'You have said I know you by name and you have found favor with me.' John 10:14-15: 'I know my sheep and my sheep know me — just as the Father knows me and I know the Father.' Christ intercedes from within the mutual divine knowing; his intercession succeeds because he is beloved of the Father (John 17:24-26).
13: A theme: 'Show me your ways, that I may know you.' The pursuit of knowing God as the heart of the covenant life. Philippians 3:8-10: 'I count everything as loss... that I may know him.' John 14:6-9: Christ is himself the answer to Moses's request — 'the one who has seen me has seen the Father.'
14: A direct type: 'My Presence will go with you, and I will give you rest.' Matthew 11:28-29: Jesus appropriates the exact language of YHWH's promise — 'Come to me, all who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest.' Hebrews 4:1-11: the rest YHWH promises here is the eschatological sabbath rest that Christ opens; he is the Presence and the rest in one person.
15: A theme: 'If your Presence does not go with us, do not bring us from here.' The indispensability of divine Presence for the covenant community's existence. John 15:5: 'Apart from me you can do nothing' — Christ makes the same claim in inverted form; without his presence the mission is not merely difficult but impossible.
16: A theme: God's Presence going with Israel is what distinguishes them from all other peoples on earth. The indwelling Spirit as the distinguishing mark of the new covenant community (Galatians 3:2-5; 1 Corinthians 12:13; Ephesians 1:13-14; Romans 8:9): not ethnicity or law-observance but Christ's Spirit within constitutes the people of God.
17: A type: YHWH grants Moses's intercession because Moses has found favor and is known by name. Hebrews 7:25: 'he always lives to make intercession for them.' John 17:9,24: Christ intercedes and is heard because the Father loves him. The successful intercession of Moses is a pattern of Christ's intercession that never fails.
18: A type: 'Show me your glory' — the deepest request of the OT's greatest intercessor. John 12:41: Isaiah saw Christ's glory and spoke about him. John 14:8-9: Philip asks 'Show us the Father' and Jesus replies 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father.' The Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1-8) is the earthly partial fulfillment; the new Jerusalem is its completion (Revelation 21:23).
19: A revelation of God: 'I will be gracious to whom I will be gracious, and show mercy to whom I will show mercy.' Paul cites Ex 33:19 in Romans 9:15 to establish the sovereign freedom of divine mercy — grace is never earned. Ephesians 2:4-9: salvation rests entirely on this freedom, 'not from works, so that no one may boast.'
20: A revelation of God: 'You cannot see my face, for no one may see me and live.' John 1:18 resolves this: 'No one has seen God at any time; the only-begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, has made him known.' Hebrews 1:3: Christ as 'the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being' — the incarnation is the answer to this terrifying impossibility. 2 Corinthians 4:6: the light of the knowledge of God's glory is now seen 'in the face of Christ.'
21: A type: 'There is a place near me where you shall stand on the rock.' 1 Corinthians 10:4: 'that Rock was Christ.' The designated place of safety near God is the Rock himself. Ephesians 2:6: believers are 'raised up with him and seated with him in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus' — to stand on the Rock is to stand in Christ.
22: A type: 'I will put you in a cleft of the rock, and cover you with my hand until I have passed by.' 1 Corinthians 10:4 identifies the Rock as Christ; Colossians 3:3: 'your life is hidden with Christ in God.' The cleft of the Rock is union with Christ; his covering hand = his righteousness sheltering the one who is in him (2 Corinthians 5:21).
23: A shadow: 'You will see my back, but my face will not be seen' — the characteristic mode of OT revelation: mediated, partial, from behind. 2 Corinthians 3:14-18: the veil lying over the old covenant. But John 14:9 and 2 Corinthians 4:6 invert this entirely: in Christ's face the full knowledge of God's glory is now directly accessible to all who turn to him.
